content.text: Motor control and actuator integration is the layer that converts high-level motion plans—sequences of waypoints or reference trajectories—into precise electrical or pneumatic commands that physically move a robot's joints, wheels, or end-effectors. It bridges the gap between

(motors, servos, pneumatic cylinders). The core challenge is tracking a desired state—position, velocity, or torque—accurately and stably in the presence of friction, inertia, disturbances, and sensor noise, all within tight real-time deadlines.

The principal control strategies are PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative), Model Predictive Control (MPC), and feedforward + feedback composites. Modern robot software stacks expose these through framework abstractions:
in ROS 2 is the dominant standard, providing a hardware-abstraction layer with real-time read/write loops that separate controller logic from vendor-specific drivers.

Even a perfect planner fails if the controller cannot faithfully execute its commands. Tracking errors accumulate into localization drift; oscillating joints damage hardware; sluggish velocity responses make collision avoidance calculations stale. Real-time control loops running at 1 kHz must complete their read–compute–write cycle within 1 ms—missing a deadline by even a few milliseconds at high controller gains can cause instability. Understanding the full stack from planned trajectory through hardware interface is therefore essential for building reliable autonomous systems.
Feedback control continuously corrects for disturbances, keeping actual motion close to the reference.
ros2_control lets you swap motor drivers without rewriting controller logic.
MPC can enforce torque, velocity, and joint-limit constraints in a principled optimization framework.
Separate trajectory generator, controller, and hardware interface layers can be independently upgraded.
Dedicated real-time loops with RT kernels ensure deterministic timing regardless of higher-level system load.
Gains tuned at one operating point may destabilize the system under different payloads or speeds.
Solving a quadratic program at 1 kHz is feasible only for short horizons or with warm-started solvers; long-horizon MPC is typically run at lower rates (100–200 Hz) with an inner PID loop.
Feedforward and MPC require accurate dynamic models; model mismatch introduces systematic errors.
Every pipeline stage adds delay; total end-to-end latency of ~38 ms limits the speed at which a robot can respond to dynamic obstacles.
PID integral term accumulates large values when the actuator is saturated, causing overshoot on recovery. Clamping or back-calculation is required.
The control pipeline has four stages:
Converts discrete waypoints from the planner into smooth, time-parameterized references
using splines (cubic, quintic) or trapezoidal velocity profiles.
Compares reference state to measured state, computes a control signal
over a bus (PWM, CAN, EtherCAT) to the motor driver.
Encoder ticks, IMU data, or force/torque readings are sampled by the hardware interface and fed back to the controller to close the loop.
is the simplest closed-loop strategy. The control signal is:

attrs.content: u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}

content.text: augments this with a model-based term that predicts the required effort from the desired trajectory, reducing the load on the feedback term:
solves a finite-horizon optimal control problem at each time step:
subject to the discrete system dynamics
and bounds on states and inputs. Only the first control input is applied (receding horizon).

content.text: When the plant dynamics are well-behaved, actuator bandwidth is high, and simplicity matters. Suitable for DC motors at fixed loads, differential-drive base velocity control.
When the desired trajectory is known ahead of time and gravity or inertia terms are significant (robot arms, quadruped legs).
When hard constraints on torque, joint limits, or obstacle avoidance must be enforced simultaneously, and a model of sufficient accuracy is available (autonomous vehicles, legged locomotion).
Any ROS 2 robot project where you want a standard hardware-abstraction layer with hot-swappable controllers and lifecycle management.
The ros2_control stack requires three artifacts: a URDF/xacro tag declaring the hardware interface, a hardware plugin implementing
, and a controller configuration YAML. The controller manager loads the hardware plugin at startup, then spawns and activates controllers via the lifecycle state machine.
The corresponding controller configuration activates the
and links it to the hardware interface:

# controllers.yaml
controller_manager:
  ros__parameters:
    update_rate: 1000  # Hz — real-time control loop frequency

    diff_drive_controller:
      type: diff_drive_controller/DiffDriveController

diff_drive_controller:
  ros__parameters:
    left_wheel_names:  ["left_wheel_joint"]
    right_wheel_names: ["right_wheel_joint"]
    wheel_separation:  0.34          # metres
    wheel_radius:      0.06          # metres
    publish_odom_tf:   true
    # Velocity PID gains (inner loop inside the controller)
    left_wheel_pid:
      p: 5.0
      i: 0.2
      d: 0.05
      i_clamp: 2.0
    right_wheel_pid:
      p: 5.0
      i: 0.2
      d: 0.05
      i_clamp: 2.0
A discrete PID update is straightforward to implement from scratch when you need a custom controller outside ros2_control:

class PIDController:
    """Discrete PID with anti-windup clamping."""

    def __init__(self, kp: float, ki: float, kd: float,
                 dt: float, u_min: float, u_max: float):
        self.kp, self.ki, self.kd = kp, ki, kd
        self.dt = dt
        self.u_min, self.u_max = u_min, u_max
        self._integral = 0.0
        self._prev_error = 0.0

    def update(self, setpoint: float, measurement: float) -> float:
        error = setpoint - measurement
        derivative = (error - self._prev_error) / self.dt

        # Proportional + derivative terms
        u = self.kp * error + self.kd * derivative

        # Accumulate integral only when not saturated (anti-windup)
        u_unsat = u + self.ki * self._integral
        if self.u_min <= u_unsat <= self.u_max:
            self._integral += error * self.dt

        u_out = float(self.kp * error
                      + self.ki * self._integral
                      + self.kd * derivative)
        u_out = max(self.u_min, min(self.u_max, u_out))
        self._prev_error = error
        return u_out


# Usage: 1 kHz velocity loop
pid = PIDController(kp=5.0, ki=0.2, kd=0.05,
                    dt=1e-3, u_min=-12.0, u_max=12.0)

dt = 1e-3  # 1 ms
for _ in range(1000):           # 1-second run
    v_meas = read_encoder_velocity()    # rad/s from hardware
    v_ref  = trajectory_generator.get_velocity()  # reference
    voltage = pid.update(v_ref, v_meas)
    send_pwm(voltage_to_pwm(voltage))
A Raspberry Pi-controlled two-wheel robot uses ros2_control with
MoveIt 2 plans a Cartesian path, generates a joint-space trajectory, and sends it to
which executes it using inner PID loops at 1 kHz.
MIT Cheetah runs a whole-body MPC at 500 Hz that outputs desired foot forces; lower-level PD controllers on each joint execute those forces at 5 kHz.
The TurtleBot 4 uses ros2_control with a

running at 62.5 Hz (the iRobot Create 3 base reports odometry at that rate). The hardware interface receives Create 3 wheel velocity commands over a custom USB protocol. Nav2's controller server generates
at the planner rate (~20 Hz), and the

interpolates between those commands. The architecture means swapping the Create 3 for a custom drive board requires only rewriting the hardware plugin—no changes to Nav2, MoveIt 2, or any higher layer.
When a motor saturates during a ramp-up, the integral accumulates unbounded. The robot then overshoots severely when the motor enters its linear range.
Running the control loop in a non-RT thread:
Scheduling jitter on a standard Linux kernel can cause loop periods of 5–50 ms instead of 1 ms. Always use an RT kernel (PREEMPT_RT) and
The derivative term amplifies high-frequency noise from encoders. Apply a low-pass filter (first-order, cutoff ~50 Hz) to the derivative or use a filtered-derivative PID formulation.
Mismatched units between planner and controller:
; the motor driver may expect RPM or encoder counts/s. A missing conversion factor silently under-actuates or over-actuates.
CAN bus or UART adds 0.5–5 ms latency. At high gains this latency creates a phase lag that causes oscillation. Model the delay explicitly or reduce controller bandwidth accordingly.
An outer position loop feeds a velocity setpoint to an inner velocity loop. Each loop runs at a different rate (outer ~50 Hz, inner ~1 kHz), with the inner loop having higher bandwidth.
Validate hardware interfaces in simulation first:
hardware plugin to test controller behavior in Gazebo before touching real hardware.
calls with timestamps to detect jitter spikes before they manifest as visible oscillation.
Step changes in the reference cause derivative spikes. Interpolate waypoints with at least a cubic spline or trapezoidal velocity profile before handing them to the controller.
until slight oscillation; back off 50 %. Add derivative to damp, then add integral to remove steady-state error.
A PID controller is O(1) per timestep—trivially real-time even on a microcontroller. MPC complexity scales with horizon length

typically solves in 0.2–2 ms on a modern CPU with OSQP, making 500 Hz loops feasible. For many-joint systems (e.g., humanoids with 30+ DoF), whole-body MPC requires efficient sparse QP solvers and dedicated CPU cores. ros2_control scales horizontally: each additional controller is a separate plugin with its own update loop, and the controller manager dispatches them concurrently on a thread pool.
C code generation; sub-millisecond QP solve for small systems
Python/MATLAB API; integrates with IPOPT, OSQP
Fast inverse dynamics for feedforward computation; URDF / SDF import
Motion planning + trajectory execution for arms
Interfaces with ros2_control via FollowJointTrajectory action
pip-installable; includes anti-windup and derivative filter options
Hardware watchdogs stop motors if the control loop fails to write within a timeout (typically 50–100 ms). This prevents motors from holding the last command indefinitely after a software crash.
lifecycle callback should cut motor power. ros2_control's lifecycle state machine ensures transitions are handled gracefully.
The hardware interface should clamp incoming commands to safe ranges before writing; never trust that a planner or controller respects physical limits.
Monitor motor temperature, current draw, and encoder sanity (velocity too high for timestep). Log anomalies and trigger controlled deceleration rather than an abrupt stop.
Motor control closes the loop between planned trajectories and physical motion; without it, even perfect planning is useless.
PID is the workhorse for velocity and position loops; feedforward reduces the burden on integral and derivative terms when a model is available.
MPC handles hard constraints at the cost of requiring an accurate dynamic model and a QP solver fast enough for the desired control rate.
ros2_control decouples controller logic from hardware drivers via a read/write interface; the controller manager enforces lifecycle transitions and runs the real-time loop.
End-to-end latency (~38 ms for a typical mobile robot) limits how quickly the system can react; using an RT kernel and isolating the control CPU to a dedicated core minimizes jitter.
Anti-windup, derivative filtering, and watchdog timers are non-negotiable in production systems.
